summary: The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special zoning workshop with the Zoning Commission, Board of Zoning Appeals, and Zoning Inspector Kevin Maylock to discuss potential amendments to the Zoning Resolution. Key topics included increasing R1 minimum lot sizes from approximately 1 acre to 5 acres, adjusting side yard setbacks, correcting an R5 lot size typo, incorporating property maintenance provisions into the zoning code under House Bill 315, and debating whether to eliminate or modify the Planned Unit Development (PUD) residential provisions. Direction was given to prepare revised lot size and setback numbers for the next regular meeting.
decisions: Direction given to draft R1 minimum lot size change to 5 acres for next meeting
Direction given to draft proposed lot sizes: R5 at 12,000 sq ft, R4 at 15,000 sq ft, R3 at 25,000 sq ft, R2 at 40,000 sq ft, R1 at 5 acres
Direction given to draft side yard setbacks: 20 feet for R1-R3 and 15 feet for R4-R5
Dan Hodges' motion for 5 acres without sewer / 3 acres with sewer was withdrawn and tabled to next meeting
Final consensus settled on a flat 5-acre R1 minimum without sewer distinction
action_items: Kevin Maylock (Zoning Inspector) to prepare revised Table 5-2 with new lot sizes and setback numbers for next trustees meeting
Kevin Maylock to work with Julia Carney at prosecutor's office on resolution language for lot size and setback changes
Kevin Maylock to prepare property maintenance code inclusion into zoning code under House Bill 315 for further review
Kevin Maylock to continue researching New Community Authority program and awaiting response from Dublin
Kevin Maylock to prepare a township zoning map showing quadrants and zoning districts for next workshop meeting
Kevin Maylock to research PUD elimination vs. minimum removal with legal counsel for future discussion
Board to continue working on land use plan updates as a future priority
key_amounts: Current R1 minimum lot size: 43,400 sq ft (~0.96 acres)
Proposed R1 minimum lot size: 5 acres
Proposed R5 minimum lot size: 12,000 sq ft (currently listed as 18,000, believed typo for 8,000)
Proposed R4 minimum lot size: 15,000 sq ft
Proposed R3 minimum lot size: 25,000 sq ft
Proposed R2 minimum lot size: 40,000 sq ft
Current PD minimum lot size: 5,000 sq ft
Average 5-acre lot in Goshen Township selling for $194,000
Average 5-acre lot in Warren County selling for $265,000
Proposed side yard setbacks: 20 feet for R1-R3, 15 feet for R4-R5
New Community Authority board: up to 13 members (7 elected, 6 developer-appointed)
Property maintenance grass height discussed at 12 inches (previously 8 inches)
notable_topics: R1 minimum lot size increase from ~1 acre to 5 acres
Restructuring minimum lot sizes for R2 through R5 zoning districts
Side yard setback increases across residential zones
R5 lot size typo correction (18,000 should be 8,000 sq ft)
Possible elimination or modification of Planned Unit Development (PUD/PDR) residential provisions
Land use plan as critical enforcement tool for zoning decisions
Property maintenance code incorporation into zoning code under House Bill 315
New Community Authority program research (House Bill 315)
History of high-density subdivision lawsuits (Cameron Crossing, Woodland Streams)
Apartments vs. condominiums for tax base purposes
Lot width minimums in relation to setback changes
attendees: Melissa (Zoning staff, last name not stated)
Julia Carney (Clermont County Prosecutor's Office, referenced but not present)
notes: Transcript was truncated before the meeting concluded - Item B regarding a resignation acceptance and zoning personnel matters were not fully captured. Multiple historical references to lawsuits over denied subdivisions (Cameron Crossing, Woodland Streams, Lakefield Place) that resulted in court-ordered high-density developments. The high-density moratorium is currently in place. There was significant debate about whether to use a sewer/no-sewer distinction for lot sizes, ultimately settling on a flat 5-acre minimum for R1. The PUD discussion was deferred but identified as a critical follow-up item due to potential conflicts with new straight zoning minimums.
title: Special BOT  Zoning Workshop 5-22-25
